talk_title: Genomics and environmental risk factors in East Asian lung adenocarcinoma

speaker.name: Takashi Kono
speaker.affiliation: National Cancer Center, Japan; Vice President of Japanese Cancer Association; Director of JCGAT

targets.dependency_context: never smoker lung adenocarcinoma, East Asian

models.name: JCGAT national database
models.genotype_or_key_features: 130,000 patient records, 7,000 lung cancer, smoking status collected

MOA_summary: Kono presented large-scale national genomic data showing distinct mutation patterns in never-smoker versus smoker lung adenocarcinoma, with EGFR mutations predominating in never smokers and KRAS in smokers, and highlighted environmental contributors such as passive smoking, air pollution, and germline polymorphisms influencing tumor development.
key_findings: Never smokers comprised ~30% of advanced lung cancer cases in the Japanese national database.
EGFR mutations are the most frequent driver in never-smoker lung adenocarcinoma, while KRAS dominates in smokers.
Passive smoking increases tumor mutational burden via APOBEC signatures but does not affect EGFR mutation frequency.
Air pollution (PM2.5) correlates with higher EGFR-positive lung cancer incidence without directly causing EGFR mutations.
Germline polymorphisms (e.g., TERT, HLA class II) confer greater risk in never smokers, especially for EGFR-positive disease.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: EGFR-positive tumors in smokers often lack SBS4 smoking signature, indicating a smoking-independent development pathway.
open_questions: What are the functional mechanisms by which identified germline variants (TERT, HLA) promote EGFR-driven tumorigenesis?
How does passive smoking interact with APOBEC activity to influence tumor evolution?

talk_title: Germline susceptibility and genomic risk in never-smoker lung cancer

biomarkers.name: Polygenic risk score for lung adenocarcinoma
biomarkers.finding: PRS associated with ~1.3-fold increased risk per SD in both young-onset and never-smoker cohorts

biomarkers.name: TP53 pathogenic variants
biomarkers.finding: Enriched in young-onset lung cancer; rare variant burden significant

biomarkers.name: SMAD6 rare variants
biomarkers.finding: Associated with non-fusion driven lung cancer

models.name: Young Lung Cancer (YLC) study
models.genotype_or_key_features: 620 patients ≤45 y, 87% never/light smokers, enriched for ALK/ROS1 fusions

models.name: INHERIT study
models.genotype_or_key_features: familial lung cancer, includes EGFR T790M carriers and other hereditary variants

clinical_data.metric: ORR to platinum + PARP inhibitor in BRCA2 germline carrier
clinical_data.value: durable response >4 y to platinum, 16 m on PARP inhibitor
clinical_data.population: stage IV BRCA2 germline mutant lung cancer

MOA_summary: Lo Piccolo described large-scale germline sequencing efforts revealing that rare pathogenic variants in established cancer genes (TP53, SMAD6, IREB2) and polygenic risk scores contribute to lung cancer risk in never smokers, especially in young-onset and EGFR‑positive disease, and highlighted a case where BRCA2 loss of heterozygosity sensitized a patient to platinum and PARP inhibition.
key_findings: Young‑onset lung cancer (≤45 y) is enriched for targetable fusions (ALK, ROS1) and EGFR mutations.
Rare pathogenic germline variants in TP53, SMAD6, and IREB2 are significantly enriched in young‑onset or fusion‑driven tumors.
Polygenic risk scores derived from smoking‑associated GWAS still confer modest risk (OR ≈ 1.3 per SD) in never‑smoker cohorts.
Large (>1 Mb) rare deletions are over‑represented in young‑onset, fusion‑positive cases.
A BRCA2 germline pathogenic variant with tumor LOH predicted exceptional response to platinum chemotherapy followed by PARP inhibition.
open_questions: How do germline variants interact with environmental exposures (e.g., air pollution) to shape somatic driver selection?
Can polygenic risk scores be refined to improve predictive power specifically for never‑smoker lung cancer?

talk_title: Differential treatment response by smoking status in lung cancer

speaker.name: Seung Lee
speaker.affiliation: Samsung Medical Center, Republic of Korea

targets.dependency_context: never smoker adenocarcinoma immune checkpoint response

biomarkers.name: PD-L1 CPS
biomarkers.finding: Higher PD-L1 associated with better ICI response but overall lower in never smokers

biomarkers.name: Tumor mutation burden (TMB)
biomarkers.finding: Never smokers have significantly lower TMB; high TMB rare but predicts ICI benefit

biomarkers.name: PBAP loss‑of‑function mutation
biomarkers.finding: ~10% of never‑smoker NSCLC; associated with favorable ICI response

biomarkers.name: DDR1 expression
biomarkers.finding: High DDR1 linked to hyperprogression on ICI in never smokers

models.name: Immuno‑Oncology Clinical Genomics cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 5,000 lung cancer patients (2,000 never smokers, 3,000 smokers) with genomic and treatment data

clinical_data.metric: EGFR TKI hazard ratio (never smoker vs smoker)

MOA_summary: Lee demonstrated that never‑smoker lung cancer shows superior benefit from EGFR TKIs but diminished response to immune checkpoint inhibitors, correlating with lower TMB and PD‑L1; he identified biomarkers (PBAP loss, DDR1, transcriptional MMR‑like signature) that predict ICI sensitivity or hyperprogression, and highlighted that anti‑angiogenic addition improves TKI efficacy mainly in smokers.
key_findings: EGFR TKI hazard ratio 0.7 in never smokers versus smokers, confirming greater efficacy.
Addition of anti‑angiogenic agents to EGFR TKI improves outcomes more in smokers than never smokers.
Never smokers have significantly lower TMB and PD‑L1 scores, resulting in poor ICI monotherapy response (ORR ~10%).
PBAP loss‑of‑function mutations (~10% of never smokers) predict durable ICI responses (5‑year remission).
High DDR1 expression associates with hyperprogression on ICI in never smokers.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: High DDR1 expression linked to impaired T‑cell infiltration and hyperprogression on ICI.
Low APOBEC signature in never smokers correlates with reduced ICI benefit.

combination_strategies.rationale: Enhance EGFR TKI efficacy; more benefit observed in smokers
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved PFS in smokers, modest effect in never smokers

combination_strategies.rationale: Target EGFR exon 20 insertions and overcome resistance
combination_strategies.phenotype: Promising responses in rare EGFR variants

open_questions: What molecular mechanisms underlie the differential benefit of anti‑angiogenic agents in smokers versus never smokers?
Can DDR1 inhibition restore ICI sensitivity in high‑DDR1 never‑smoker tumors?

talk_title: Molecular evolution and prognostic classification of never‑smoker lung adenocarcinoma (Sherlock study)

targets.dependency_context: co‑occurs with EGFR, drives genomic instability

biomarkers.name: ID2 indel signature (T homopolymer deletions)
biomarkers.finding: Higher ID2 count correlates with aggressive proliferation markers

biomarkers.name: SBS5 clock‑like signature
biomarkers.finding: Enriched in tumors from high PM2.5 regions

biomarkers.name: Methylation‑based AT1 vs AT2 cell‑of‑origin classifier
biomarkers.finding: AT1‑origin signature predicts low‑grade, indolent tumors

biomarkers.name: 60‑gene transcriptomic prognostic signature
biomarkers.finding: Distinguishes steady (alveolar) vs proliferative (fusion) subtypes with significant OS differences

models.name: Sherlock study
models.genotype_or_key_features: ≈5,000 individuals, ~3,000 with multi‑omics; includes never smokers and smokers

MOA_summary: Landi described comprehensive whole‑genome and transcriptomic profiling of ~1,200 lung adenocarcinomas, revealing that never‑smoker tumors follow distinct evolutionary paths: EGFR‑mutant, copy‑number‑rich, AT1‑cell‑origin cancers evolve slowly, whereas KRKR‑mutant, low‑CNV tumors arise rapidly; environmental exposures (PM2.5, indoor coal) imprint specific mutational signatures, and integrated methylation and expression signatures provide prognostic stratification.
key_findings: Three genomic subtypes (piano, mezzo‑forte, forte) correlate with copy‑number burden and driver mutations.
Endogenous signatures (SBS1, SBS5, APOBEC) dominate; exogenous SBS4 and Signature 22A (aristolochic acid) appear in specific regions.
High outdoor PM2.5 exposure associates with increased SBS5 and reduced telomere length.
KRAS‑mutant tumors have shorter latency (≈5 y) and earlier age at diagnosis compared with EGFR‑mutant tumors (≈6 y).
AT1‑origin methylation signature distinguishes low‑grade, indolent tumors and aligns with the steady transcriptomic group.
open_questions: How does the AT1‑cell epigenetic constraint influence response to targeted therapies?
Can the methylation‑based cell‑of‑origin classifier be applied prospectively for early detection?
